expanded_description: Understanding how to draw Lewis structures for complex molecules that go beyond simple octet rule applications, including resonance structures, formal charge calculations, expanded octets, incomplete octets, and polyatomic ions.

core_concept.principle: Lewis structures for complex molecules require systematic application of electron counting, formal charge minimization, resonance considerations, and recognition of octet rule exceptions to accurately represent bonding and electron distribution.
core_concept.explanation: Complex molecules often violate simple Lewis structure rules through resonance (multiple valid structures), expanded octets (central atoms from period 3+ with d orbitals), incomplete octets (electron-deficient atoms), or unusual bonding patterns. A systematic approach considering valence electrons, octet requirements, formal charges, and resonance enables accurate structure determination for molecules like sulfate (SO₄²⁻), nitrate (NO₃⁻), benzene (C₆H₆), and compounds with central atoms from period 3 or below.

image.alt: Diagram showing various complex Lewis structures including resonance, expanded octets, and formal charges
image.caption: Complex Lewis structures require systematic approach considering resonance, formal charge, and octet exceptions

sections.introduction: Before tackling complex molecules, it's essential to master the fundamental rules for drawing Lewis structures. These rules form the foundation for handling more challenging cases involving resonance, formal charge, and octet exceptions.

key_concept.definition: Lewis structures represent valence electrons as dots or lines, showing bonding pairs (shared electrons) and lone pairs (unshared electrons) to satisfy the octet rule for most atoms.
key_concept.context: Basic rules work for most period 2 elements but require modifications for complex molecules with resonance, expanded octets, or unusual bonding.

principles.statement: Total valence electrons = sum of valence electrons from all atoms + electrons from negative charges - electrons from positive charges.
principles.application: First step in drawing any Lewis structure; ensures correct total electron count before distributing electrons.
principles.analogy: Like counting total budget before spending: know your total electrons before distributing them.

principles.statement: Most atoms (especially C, N, O, F) achieve stability with 8 valence electrons (4 electron pairs) through bonding and lone pairs.
principles.application: Guide for electron distribution; exceptions exist for H (2e⁻), period 3+ elements (can expand), and electron-deficient atoms.
principles.example: CH₄: C has 4 bonds (8e⁻); each H has 1 bond (2e⁻); all atoms satisfy their electron requirements
principles.analogy: Like filling seats at a table: most atoms want 8 'seats' (electrons), H only wants 2.

principles.statement: Connect atoms with single bonds first, then add lone pairs to complete octets, then form multiple bonds if needed to satisfy octets.
principles.application: Systematic approach prevents errors; ensures all electrons are accounted for before forming multiple bonds.
principles.example: CO₂: Start C-O-O with single bonds (16e⁻ used); add lone pairs; C needs 8e⁻ but only has 4; form C=O double bonds
principles.analogy: Like building a house: foundation (single bonds) first, then walls (lone pairs), then upgrades (multiple bonds) if needed.

classifications.value: Sum all valence electrons from atoms and adjust for ionic charge
characteristics: Group 1: 1e⁻; Group 2: 2e⁻; Group 13: 3e⁻; Group 14: 4e⁻
Group 15: 5e⁻; Group 16: 6e⁻; Group 17: 7e⁻; Group 18: 8e⁻
Add 1e⁻ per negative charge; subtract 1e⁻ per positive charge
classifications.behavior: Total valence electrons determines how many electrons available for bonding and lone pairs

examples.explanation: N (5e⁻) + 4×H (4×1 = 4e⁻) - 1e⁻ (positive charge) = 8 valence electrons
P (5e⁻) + 4×O (4×6 = 24e⁻) + 3e⁻ (negative charge) = 32 valence electrons

classifications.value: Connect atoms with single bonds; central atom is usually least electronegative (except H)
characteristics: H always terminal (forms 1 bond only)
Most electronegative atoms usually terminal
Central atom often has highest bonding capacity
Symmetrical arrangements often preferred
classifications.behavior: Skeleton determines connectivity; can't change without changing molecule identity

examples.explanation: S central (less EN than O); 4 O atoms around S; 2 H atoms bonded to 2 of the O atoms
2 C atoms bonded; 3 H on first C; O-O-H on second C (carboxylic acid group)

classifications.value: Add lone pairs to complete octets, starting with terminal atoms
characteristics: Fill terminal atom octets first
Central atom octet last
H atoms never get lone pairs (max 2e⁻)
Count electrons as you add them
classifications.behavior: Ensures efficient electron use; terminal atoms typically more electronegative and 'want' electrons more

examples.explanation: After 3 N-O single bonds (6e⁻), 26e⁻ remain; fill O octets first (18e⁻); 8e⁻ left for N

classifications.type: Step 4: Form Multiple Bonds if Needed
classifications.value: If central atom lacks octet, convert lone pairs from terminal atoms to bonding pairs
characteristics: Move lone pair from terminal atom to form double/triple bond
Check that all atoms satisfy octet (or appropriate requirement)
Verify total electron count unchanged
Calculate formal charges to verify best structure
classifications.behavior: Multiple bonds allow central atom to achieve octet without adding electrons

examples.explanation: C has only 4e⁻ with single bonds; convert one lone pair from each O to form C=O double bonds; C now has 8e⁻
Each N has 5 valence e⁻; single bond leaves each N with 6e⁻; form triple bond for 8e⁻ each

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Nitrate Ion (NO₃⁻)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for the nitrate ion (NO₃⁻), showing all valence electrons.

step_by_step.explanation: N (Group 15) = 5e⁻; 3×O (Group 16) = 3×6 = 18e⁻; -1 charge = +1e⁻
N is central (less electronegative than O); connect 3 O atoms to N with single bonds

step_by_step.step: Add lone pairs to terminal atoms
step_by_step.explanation: Add 3 lone pairs (6e⁻) to each O atom to complete their octets
N has only 3 bonds (6e⁻); needs 8e⁻ for octet; must form multiple bond
Convert one lone pair from one O to form N=O double bond
step_by_step.calculation: N now has 4 bonds (8e⁻); octet satisfied

step_by_step.explanation: Enclose structure in brackets with -1 charge indicated

worked_examples.final_answer: NO₃⁻: N central with one N=O double bond and two N-O single bonds; each O has complete octet; total 24 valence electrons; -1 charge
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic 4-step approach: count electrons, draw skeleton, distribute electrons, form multiple bonds if needed.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), showing all atoms and bonds.

step_by_step.explanation: S central; 4 O atoms around S; 2 H atoms bonded to 2 of the O atoms (acidic H)

step_by_step.step: Add lone pairs to O atoms
step_by_step.explanation: 2 O atoms bonded to H need 2 lone pairs each; 2 O atoms not bonded to H need 3 lone pairs each
S has 4 bonds (8e⁻); octet satisfied; no multiple bonds needed for basic structure
Calculate formal charges to determine if structure can be improved (covered in next section)
step_by_step.calculation: Basic structure complete; formal charge optimization may add S=O double bonds

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂SO₄: S central with 4 O atoms; 2 O atoms bonded to H; all atoms have complete octets; 32 valence electrons total
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acidic H atoms bond to O atoms, not directly to central atom; systematic electron distribution ensures all atoms satisfied.

sections.key_concept: Formal charge is a bookkeeping tool that helps determine the most stable Lewis structure when multiple valid structures exist. The best structure minimizes formal charges and places negative charges on more electronegative atoms.

categories.description: Formal charge = valence electrons - (lone pair electrons + ½ bonding electrons)
categories.detailed_mechanism: FC = V - (L + B/2), where V = valence electrons in free atom, L = lone pair electrons, B = bonding electrons

examples.explanation: V = 5 (N valence); L = 0 (no lone pairs); B = 8 (4 bonds × 2e⁻); FC = 5 - (0 + 4) = +1
V = 6 (O valence); L = 4 (2 lone pairs); B = 4 (2 bonds × 2e⁻); FC = 6 - (4 + 2) = 0

categories.description: Rules for evaluating Lewis structures using formal charges
categories.detailed_mechanism: Best structure has: (1) formal charges closest to zero, (2) negative charges on more electronegative atoms, (3) minimal charge separation

examples.explanation: O=C=O structure: all FC = 0 (best); O-C≡O structure: FC on O = -1, C = +1, O = 0 (worse)
Structure with negative charge on O (most EN) is most stable; negative charge on C (least EN) is least stable

categories.description: Formal charge and oxidation state are different electron-counting methods with different purposes
categories.detailed_mechanism: Formal charge: assumes equal electron sharing in bonds; Oxidation state: assumes electrons belong to more electronegative atom

examples.explanation: Formal charge = 0 (electrons shared equally); Oxidation state = +4 (electrons assigned to O)
Formal charge = 0; Oxidation state = -4 (electrons assigned to C, more EN than H)

classifications.value: Step-by-step method for determining formal charge on any atom
characteristics: Step 1: Count valence electrons in free atom (V)
Step 2: Count lone pair electrons on atom (L)
Step 3: Count bonding electrons and divide by 2 (B/2)
Step 4: FC = V - (L + B/2)
classifications.behavior: Sum of all formal charges must equal overall molecular/ionic charge

examples.explanation: V = 6; L = 0; B = 12 (6 bonds in expanded octet structure); FC = 6 - (0 + 6) = 0
Single-bonded O: V = 6; L = 6; B = 2; FC = 6 - (6 + 1) = -1; Double-bonded O: V = 6; L = 4; B = 4; FC = 6 - (4 + 2) = 0

classifications.value: Using formal charges to determine best Lewis structure among alternatives
characteristics: Structures with FC closest to zero are most stable
Negative FC should be on more electronegative atoms
Minimize charge separation (avoid + and - on adjacent atoms)
Sum of FC must equal molecular/ionic charge
classifications.behavior: Formal charge analysis guides selection of most realistic structure

examples.explanation: All three resonance structures equivalent; N has FC = +1; one O has FC = -1; two O have FC = 0; sum = -1 ✓
H-O-Cl (FC all 0) better than H-Cl-O (FC on Cl = +1, O = -1); first structure observed

classifications.value: Quick methods for estimating formal charges without full calculation
characteristics: Each bond contributes -1 to FC (½ × 2e⁻)
Each lone pair contributes -2 to FC
FC = V - (number of lines + number of dots)
Common patterns: N with 4 bonds = +1; O with 1 bond = -1
classifications.behavior: Shortcuts enable rapid structure evaluation during problem-solving

examples.explanation: V = 5; 4 bonds (4 lines); 0 lone pairs (0 dots); FC = 5 - 4 = +1
V = 6; 3 bonds (3 lines); 1 lone pair (2 dots); FC = 6 - (3 + 2) = +1

worked_examples.title: Calculating Formal Charges for NO₃⁻
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the formal charge on each atom in the nitrate ion (NO₃⁻) and verify the sum equals the ionic charge.

step_by_step.explanation: N central with one N=O double bond and two N-O single bonds; each O has complete octet
V = 5 (N valence); L = 0 (no lone pairs); B = 8 (4 bonds × 2e⁻)
V = 6 (O valence); L = 4 (2 lone pairs); B = 4 (2 bonds × 2e⁻)
V = 6 (O valence); L = 6 (3 lone pairs); B = 2 (1 bond × 2e⁻)
Sum all formal charges: (+1) + (0) + (-1) + (-1) = -1

worked_examples.final_answer: N: +1; double-bonded O: 0; each single-bonded O: -1; sum = -1 (matches ionic charge)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Formal charge calculation verifies Lewis structure correctness; sum must equal molecular/ionic charge.

worked_examples.title: Choosing Best Structure Using Formal Charge
worked_examples.problem: Two possible Lewis structures exist for N₂O: N-N-O or N-O-N. Use formal charge to determine which is more stable.

step_by_step.explanation: N≡N-O with formal charges: terminal N = 0, central N = +1, O = -1
N=O=N with formal charges: terminal N = -1, O = +1, terminal N = 0
N-N-O: negative charge on O (more EN); N-O-N: positive charge on O (more EN)
step_by_step.calculation: Negative charge should be on more electronegative atom

step_by_step.explanation: N-N-O structure places negative charge on O (EN = 3.44); N-O-N places positive charge on O
step_by_step.calculation: N-N-O more stable (negative on more EN atom)

step_by_step.explanation: N-N-O connectivity observed experimentally; formal charge analysis correctly predicts stability

worked_examples.final_answer: N-N-O structure is more stable because it places negative formal charge on oxygen (more electronegative) rather than positive charge; matches experimental observation
worked_examples.concept_applied: Formal charge analysis predicts molecular structure; negative charges should be on more electronegative atoms.

sections.key_concept: Resonance occurs when multiple valid Lewis structures can be drawn for a molecule by moving electrons (not atoms). The actual molecule is a resonance hybrid of all valid structures, with properties intermediate between the contributing structures.

process_flow.description: Look for multiple bonds adjacent to atoms with lone pairs, or multiple equivalent positions for double bonds.
Move only electrons (π bonds and lone pairs), not atoms; maintain same atomic connectivity.
Calculate formal charge for each atom in each resonance structure.
Structures with lower formal charges and negative charges on more EN atoms contribute more to hybrid.
Represent hybrid with dashed bonds or partial charges to show electron delocalization.

classifications.value: Structures with identical formal charge distributions contribute equally to the hybrid
characteristics: Same energy
Same formal charge pattern
Equal contribution to hybrid
Bond lengths intermediate between single and double
classifications.behavior: Actual molecule has symmetrical bond lengths and properties

examples.explanation: 3 equivalent structures; each N-O bond is 1⅓ bond order; all N-O bonds equal length
3 equivalent structures; each C-O bond is 1⅓ bond order; all C-O bonds equal length
2 equivalent structures; each C-C bond is 1½ bond order; all C-C bonds equal length

classifications.value: Structures with different formal charge distributions contribute unequally to the hybrid
characteristics: Different energies
Different formal charge patterns
Major contributor has lower energy
Minor contributor has higher energy
classifications.behavior: Actual molecule more closely resembles major contributor

examples.explanation: Structure with negative charge on O (most EN) is major contributor; structure with negative charge on C is minor
Neutral structure (all FC = 0) is major; charged structure is minor contributor

classifications.value: Rules for drawing valid resonance structures
characteristics: Only electrons move (π bonds and lone pairs)
Atoms never move (same connectivity)
Octet rule must be satisfied (for period 2)
Total electron count unchanged
Net charge unchanged
classifications.behavior: Violating these rules creates invalid structures that don't contribute to resonance

examples.explanation: Moving π electrons from C=O to create C-O⁻ with adjacent C⁺
Moving H atom from one O to another changes connectivity; not resonance

classifications.value: Methods for representing the actual molecule as a hybrid of resonance structures
characteristics: Double-headed arrow (↔) between structures
Dashed bonds for partial bond character
δ+ and δ- for partial charges
Bond order notation (e.g., 1⅓)
classifications.behavior: Hybrid representation shows delocalized electrons and intermediate properties

examples.explanation: Circle inside hexagon represents delocalized π electrons; all C-C bonds equivalent
Dashed lines show partial double bond character; each N-O bond identical

worked_examples.title: Drawing Resonance Structures for Ozone (O₃)
worked_examples.problem: Draw all resonance structures for ozone (O₃) and determine the bond order of each O-O bond.

step_by_step.explanation: 3×O = 3×6 = 18 valence electrons
O-O=O with lone pairs; central O has +1 FC; terminal double-bonded O has 0 FC; terminal single-bonded O has -1 FC
O=O-O (reverse of first); double bond on other side
Both structures have same formal charge pattern; contribute equally to hybrid
Each O-O bond is single in one structure, double in other; average = (1 + 2)/2 = 1.5
step_by_step.calculation: Bond order = 1.5 for each O-O bond

step_by_step.explanation: Show both O-O bonds as identical with partial double bond character
step_by_step.calculation: O-O bond length intermediate between single and double

worked_examples.final_answer: O₃ has 2 equivalent resonance structures; each O-O bond has bond order 1.5; actual molecule has two identical O-O bonds with length between single and double bond
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance structures with equivalent formal charges contribute equally; bond order is average of contributing structures.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Resonance Contributors for SCN⁻
worked_examples.problem: Draw the three resonance structures for thiocyanate ion (SCN⁻) and rank them by contribution to the resonance hybrid.

step_by_step.explanation: S (6e⁻) + C (4e⁻) + N (5e⁻) + 1e⁻ (charge) = 16 valence electrons
S with 3 lone pairs (-1 FC); C with 4 bonds (0 FC); N with 1 lone pair (0 FC)
S with 2 lone pairs (0 FC); C with 4 bonds (0 FC); N with 2 lone pairs (-1 FC)
S with 1 lone pair (+1 FC); C with 4 bonds (0 FC); N with 3 lone pairs (-2 FC)
Structure 2: negative on N (EN = 3.04); Structure 1: negative on S (EN = 2.58); Structure 3: -2 on N (high charge separation)
Structure 2 (S=C=N⁻) is major contributor; negative charge on most electronegative atom (N)

worked_examples.final_answer: Three resonance structures: (1) S-C≡N⁻, (2) S=C=N⁻, (3) S≡C-N²⁻; ranked by contribution: 2 > 1 > 3; Structure 2 is major contributor (negative charge on most EN atom)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance structures with negative charge on more electronegative atoms contribute more to the hybrid; minimize charge separation.

sections.key_concept: While the octet rule works for most period 2 elements, many important molecules violate it through incomplete octets (fewer than 8 electrons), expanded octets (more than 8 electrons), or odd-electron species (free radicals).

components.definition: Octet rule exceptions occur when atoms have fewer than 8 valence electrons (incomplete), more than 8 valence electrons (expanded), or odd numbers of electrons (radicals).
components.interpretation: Understanding exceptions is essential for drawing accurate Lewis structures for molecules like BF₃, SF₆, NO, and many important compounds.

image.alt: Diagram showing incomplete octet (BF₃), expanded octet (SF₆), and odd-electron (NO) examples
image.caption: Octet rule exceptions: incomplete (BF₃, 6e⁻ on B), expanded (SF₆, 12e⁻ on S), odd-electron (NO, 11 valence e⁻)

principles.statement: Some atoms, particularly Be, B, and Al, are stable with fewer than 8 valence electrons due to their low electronegativity and small size.
principles.application: Explains stability of electron-deficient compounds like BF₃ (6e⁻ on B), BeH₂ (4e⁻ on Be), and AlCl₃ (6e⁻ on Al).
principles.example: BF₃: B has only 6 valence electrons (3 bonds); molecule is stable despite incomplete octet; acts as Lewis acid
principles.analogy: Like a small table that only needs 6 chairs instead of 8; some atoms are stable with fewer electrons.

principles.statement: Atoms from period 3 or below can accommodate more than 8 valence electrons by using empty d orbitals for bonding.
principles.application: Explains molecules like SF₆ (12e⁻ on S), PCl₅ (10e⁻ on P), and XeF₄ (12e⁻ on Xe) that exceed the octet rule.
principles.example: SF₆: S has 6 bonds (12e⁻); possible because S is period 3 with available 3d orbitals
principles.analogy: Like a large table that can seat more than 8 people; larger atoms have more 'seats' (orbitals) available.

principles.statement: Molecules with odd numbers of valence electrons (free radicals) cannot satisfy the octet rule for all atoms.
principles.application: Explains reactivity of radicals like NO (11e⁻), NO₂ (17e⁻), and ClO₂ (19e⁻) that have unpaired electrons.
principles.example: NO: 11 valence electrons total; N has 7e⁻, O has 8e⁻; unpaired electron on N makes molecule highly reactive
principles.analogy: Like trying to pair an odd number of socks; one electron must remain unpaired.

classifications.value: Molecules where central atom has fewer than 8 valence electrons
characteristics: Common for Group 2 (Be: 4e⁻) and Group 13 (B, Al: 6e⁻)
Often act as Lewis acids (electron pair acceptors)
Can form coordinate covalent bonds to complete octet
Highly reactive in some cases
classifications.behavior: Electron-deficient atoms seek additional electron density through reactions

examples.explanation: B has 3 bonds (6e⁻); incomplete octet; reacts with NH₃ to form BF₃·NH₃ adduct with complete octet
Be has 2 bonds (4e⁻); highly electron-deficient; polymerizes to share electrons
Al has 3 bonds (6e⁻); dimerizes to Al₂Cl₆ to share electrons and complete octets

classifications.value: Molecules where central atom has more than 8 valence electrons
characteristics: Only period 3+ elements (have d orbitals available)
Common for S, P, Cl, Xe as central atoms
Can have 10, 12, or even 14 valence electrons
Minimize formal charges often requires expanded octet
classifications.behavior: Expanded octets allow lower formal charges and more stable structures

examples.explanation: S has 6 bonds (12e⁻); octahedral geometry; all F atoms satisfy octet
P has 5 bonds (10e⁻); trigonal bipyramidal geometry; stable molecule
Xe has 4 bonds + 2 lone pairs (12e⁻); square planar geometry; noble gas compound
S has 6 bonds in best structure (12e⁻); minimizes formal charges compared to octet structure

classifications.value: Molecules with odd numbers of valence electrons and unpaired electrons
characteristics: Total valence electrons is odd number
At least one atom has incomplete octet
Highly reactive due to unpaired electron
Often paramagnetic
classifications.behavior: Radicals seek to pair unpaired electron through reactions; often short-lived intermediates

examples.explanation: 11 valence electrons; N has 7e⁻, O has 8e⁻; unpaired electron on N; important biological signaling molecule
17 valence electrons; N has 7e⁻; brown gas; dimerizes to N₂O₄ to pair electrons
19 valence electrons; Cl has 7e⁻; used as bleach and disinfectant; reactive radical

classifications.value: Guidelines for deciding when expanded octets are appropriate
characteristics: Central atom must be period 3 or below
Expanded octet reduces formal charges
Experimental evidence supports expanded structure
Not all period 3+ molecules need expanded octets
classifications.behavior: Use expanded octet when it significantly reduces formal charges or matches experimental data

examples.explanation: Octet structure: S has FC = +2; Expanded structure (2 S=O bonds): S has FC = 0; expanded preferred
PCl₃: P has octet (8e⁻), FC = 0; no need to expand; PCl₅: P expands to 10e⁻ to bond with 5 Cl

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for SF₆ (Expanded Octet)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆) and explain why sulfur can have an expanded octet.

step_by_step.explanation: S central (less electronegative than F); connect 6 F atoms to S with single bonds

step_by_step.step: Add lone pairs to F atoms
step_by_step.explanation: Add 3 lone pairs (6e⁻) to each F atom to complete their octets
S has 6 bonds (12e⁻); exceeds octet; this is allowed for period 3+ elements
S is period 3 with empty 3d orbitals available for bonding; can accommodate more than 8 electrons
step_by_step.calculation: Expanded octet possible for period 3+ elements

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₆: S central with 6 S-F single bonds; S has 12 valence electrons (expanded octet); all atoms have FC = 0; S can expand octet because it's period 3 with available d orbitals
worked_examples.concept_applied: Period 3+ elements can expand octet using d orbitals; expanded octets often minimize formal charges.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for NO (Odd-Electron Molecule)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for nitric oxide (NO) and explain why it violates the octet rule.

step_by_step.explanation: N (Group 15) = 5e⁻; O (Group 16) = 6e⁻
Connect N and O with a bond; odd electron count means octet rule cannot be satisfied for both atoms
Add lone pairs to complete O octet first (more electronegative); remaining electron goes on N
step_by_step.calculation: O gets 3 lone pairs (6e⁻); 3e⁻ left; N gets 1 lone pair (2e⁻) + 1 unpaired electron

step_by_step.step: Form multiple bond to minimize formal charges
step_by_step.explanation: Form N=O double bond; N has 7e⁻ (5 from bonds + 2 from lone pair); O has 8e⁻

step_by_step.calculation: FC: N = +1, O = 0; sum = +1 (but molecule is neutral; unpaired electron accounts for difference)

step_by_step.explanation: Odd electron count (11e⁻) makes it impossible for both atoms to have octets; one atom must have incomplete octet
step_by_step.calculation: NO is a free radical with unpaired electron

worked_examples.final_answer: NO: N=O double bond with unpaired electron on N; N has 7 valence electrons (incomplete octet); O has 8 valence electrons (complete octet); odd electron count makes octet rule impossible to satisfy for both atoms
worked_examples.concept_applied: Odd-electron molecules (radicals) cannot satisfy octet rule for all atoms; one atom must have incomplete octet.

sections.key_concept: A systematic 7-step approach ensures accurate Lewis structures for complex molecules, incorporating electron counting, skeleton drawing, electron distribution, formal charge analysis, resonance consideration, and octet exception recognition.

process_flow.description: Sum valence electrons from all atoms; add electrons for negative charges; subtract for positive charges.
Identify central atom (usually least electronegative, except H); draw skeleton with single bonds.
Add lone pairs to complete octets of terminal atoms first; H atoms never get lone pairs.

process_flow.process: Place remaining electrons on central atom
process_flow.description: Add remaining electrons as lone pairs on central atom; check if central atom has octet.
If central atom lacks octet, convert lone pairs from terminal atoms to bonding pairs (double/triple bonds).
Calculate formal charges; adjust structure (expanded octet, multiple bonds) to minimize formal charges.
process_flow.result: Optimized structure with minimal formal charges

process_flow.process: Check for resonance and octet exceptions
process_flow.description: Identify resonance structures; recognize incomplete/expanded octets; draw resonance hybrid if applicable.
process_flow.result: Final Lewis structure(s) with resonance if applicable

classifications.value: Electron counting and connectivity determination
characteristics: Accurate electron count essential for all subsequent steps
Correct connectivity cannot be changed later
H always terminal; most EN atoms usually terminal
Central atom often has highest bonding capacity
classifications.behavior: Errors in these steps propagate through entire structure; double-check before proceeding

examples.explanation: S central; 4 O around S; 2 H bonded to 2 O (not to S directly)

classifications.value: Systematic electron placement to satisfy octets
characteristics: Terminal atoms first (more electronegative)
Central atom last
Count electrons as you add them
Track remaining electrons
classifications.behavior: Ensures efficient electron use; prevents running out of electrons before all atoms satisfied

examples.explanation: After 4 S-O bonds (8e⁻), 24e⁻ remain; add 6e⁻ to each O (24e⁻); 0e⁻ left for S

classifications.value: Multiple bonds and formal charge minimization
characteristics: Form multiple bonds to satisfy central atom octet
Calculate formal charges
Expand octet if it reduces formal charges
Negative FC on more EN atoms
classifications.behavior: Produces most stable, realistic structure; may require expanded octet for period 3+ central atoms

examples.explanation: All single bonds: S FC = +2; Add 2 S=O double bonds: S FC = 0; expanded octet preferred

classifications.value: Resonance and exception recognition
characteristics: Look for multiple valid electron arrangements
Check for incomplete/expanded octets
Identify odd-electron species
Draw resonance hybrid if applicable
classifications.behavior: Ensures complete understanding of molecule's electronic structure; identifies delocalization

examples.explanation: 3 equivalent resonance structures; draw all 3 with double-headed arrows; show resonance hybrid

worked_examples.title: Complete Systematic Approach for ClO₄⁻ (Perchlorate Ion)
worked_examples.problem: Use the 7-step systematic approach to draw the Lewis structure for perchlorate ion (ClO₄⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: Cl (7e⁻) + 4×O (24e⁻) + 1e⁻ (charge) = 32 valence electrons
Cl central (less EN than O); 4 O atoms around Cl
Add 3 lone pairs (6e⁻) to each O atom
No electrons remaining; Cl has 4 bonds (8e⁻); octet satisfied
step_by_step.calculation: Cl octet complete with single bonds

step_by_step.step: Step 5: Form multiple bonds if needed
step_by_step.explanation: Cl has octet; check formal charges before deciding on multiple bonds

step_by_step.step: Step 6: Calculate and minimize formal charges
step_by_step.explanation: All single bonds: Cl FC = 7 - (0 + 4) = +3; each O FC = 6 - (6 + 1) = -1; sum = -1 ✓ but high charge separation
step_by_step.calculation: High FC on Cl (+3); consider expanded octet

step_by_step.step: Step 6 continued: Optimize with expanded octet
step_by_step.explanation: Form 3 Cl=O double bonds; Cl FC = 7 - (0 + 7) = 0; 3 double-bonded O FC = 0; 1 single-bonded O FC = -1
step_by_step.calculation: FC: Cl = 0, 3 O = 0, 1 O = -1; sum = -1 ✓; minimized

step_by_step.explanation: 4 equivalent resonance structures (double bond can be on any of 4 O atoms); Cl has expanded octet (14e⁻)
step_by_step.calculation: Resonance hybrid with 4 equivalent structures; each Cl-O bond order = 1.75

worked_examples.final_answer: ClO₄⁻: Cl central with 3 Cl=O double bonds and 1 Cl-O single bond in each resonance structure; 4 equivalent resonance structures; Cl has expanded octet (14e⁻); each Cl-O bond order = 1.75; formal charges minimized (Cl = 0, one O = -1)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic 7-step approach ensures accurate Lewis structure; expanded octet minimizes formal charges; resonance distributes charge evenly.

worked_examples.title: Complete Systematic Approach for XeF₄ (Xenon Tetrafluoride)
worked_examples.problem: Use the 7-step systematic approach to draw the Lewis structure for xenon tetrafluoride (XeF₄).

step_by_step.explanation: Xe (8e⁻) + 4×F (28e⁻) = 36 valence electrons
Xe central (noble gas, but forms compounds); 4 F atoms around Xe
Add 3 lone pairs (6e⁻) to each F atom
Add remaining 4e⁻ as 2 lone pairs on Xe
step_by_step.calculation: Xe has 4 bonds + 2 lone pairs = 12e⁻ (expanded octet)

step_by_step.step: Step 5: Form multiple bonds if needed
step_by_step.explanation: Xe has 12e⁻ (expanded octet); all F have octets; no multiple bonds needed
No resonance (all bonds equivalent); Xe has expanded octet (12e⁻); square planar molecular geometry
step_by_step.calculation: Expanded octet structure confirmed; no resonance

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₄: Xe central with 4 Xe-F single bonds and 2 lone pairs; Xe has expanded octet (12e⁻); all FC = 0; square planar geometry; no resonance
worked_examples.concept_applied: Noble gases can form compounds with expanded octets; systematic approach handles unusual cases like noble gas compounds.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about Lewis structures for complex molecules can lead to incorrect structures. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of electron distribution and bonding.

classifications.value: Miscounting total valence electrons, especially for ions
characteristics: Forgetting to add/subtract electrons for ionic charge
Using wrong group number for valence electrons
Counting core electrons instead of valence
classifications.behavior: Always: Group number = valence electrons for main group; add 1e⁻ per negative charge; subtract 1e⁻ per positive charge

examples.explanation: Wrong: 5 + 4 = 9e⁻; Correct: 5 + 4 - 1 = 8e⁻ (subtract 1 for + charge)
Wrong: 6 + 24 = 30e⁻; Correct: 6 + 24 + 2 = 32e⁻ (add 2 for -2 charge)

classifications.value: Choosing incorrect central atom for skeleton structure
characteristics: Putting H in center (H always terminal)
Putting most electronegative atom in center
Not considering bonding capacity
classifications.behavior: Central atom: usually least electronegative (except H); highest bonding capacity; can form multiple bonds

examples.explanation: Wrong: H-O-H (H in center); Correct: O central with 2 H bonded to it
Wrong: Cl-Cl-O; Correct: O central (can form 2 bonds) with 2 Cl bonded to it

classifications.value: Not using formal charge to evaluate and optimize structures
characteristics: Accepting first structure drawn
Not checking if formal charges can be minimized
Missing expanded octet opportunities
classifications.behavior: Always calculate formal charges; adjust structure to minimize FC; consider expanded octet for period 3+ central atoms

examples.explanation: Wrong: all single bonds (S FC = +2); Correct: 2 S=O double bonds (S FC = 0, expanded octet)
Wrong: all single bonds (P FC = +1); Correct: 1 P=O double bond (P FC = 0, expanded octet)

classifications.value: Confusing resonance with equilibrium or thinking molecules switch between structures
characteristics: Thinking molecule exists as one structure then another
Drawing equilibrium arrows (⇌) instead of resonance arrows (↔)
Not recognizing resonance hybrid
classifications.behavior: Resonance: single molecule with delocalized electrons; hybrid of all structures; use double-headed arrows (↔) not equilibrium arrows

examples.explanation: Wrong: molecule switches between 3 structures; Correct: molecule is hybrid of all 3 structures simultaneously
Wrong: alternating single/double bonds; Correct: all C-C bonds identical with 1½ bond order

classifications.value: Expanding octet for period 2 elements or not expanding when appropriate
characteristics: Giving C, N, O, F more than 8 electrons
Not expanding octet for S, P, Cl when it reduces formal charges
Confusing when expanded octets are allowed
classifications.behavior: Period 2 (C, N, O, F): never expand octet (no d orbitals); Period 3+ (S, P, Cl, etc.): can expand when it reduces formal charges

examples.explanation: Wrong: C with 5 bonds (10e⁻); Correct: C with 4 bonds (8e⁻); C is period 2, cannot expand
Wrong: S with octet (can't bond to 6 F); Correct: S with 12e⁻ (expanded octet); S is period 3, can expand

classifications.value: Moving atoms instead of only electrons in resonance structures
characteristics: Changing atomic connectivity between resonance structures
Moving H atoms between positions
Creating different molecules instead of resonance
classifications.behavior: Resonance: only electrons move (π bonds and lone pairs); atoms never move; connectivity stays same

examples.explanation: Wrong: moving H from one O to another; Correct: only move π electrons between C-O bonds
Wrong: moving O atoms to different positions; Correct: only move π electrons; N-O connectivity unchanged

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Lewis Structures for Complex Molecules
sections.introduction: Test your understanding of complex Lewis structures with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces systematic approach to Lewis structures including formal charge, resonance, and octet exceptions.
key_concept.context: Focus on applying 7-step systematic approach to various complex molecules.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for the phosphate ion (PO₄³⁻), showing all resonance structures and calculating formal charges.

step_by_step.explanation: P (5e⁻) + 4×O (24e⁻) + 3e⁻ (charge) = 32 valence electrons
P central with 4 O atoms; 4 P-O bonds (8e⁻); 24e⁻ to O atoms (6e⁻ each)
Form 1 P=O double bond; P: FC = 5 - (0 + 5) = 0; double-bonded O: FC = 0; 3 single-bonded O: FC = -1 each
4 equivalent structures (double bond can be on any of 4 O atoms)

worked_examples.final_answer: PO₄³⁻: P central with 1 P=O and 3 P-O bonds in each resonance structure; 4 equivalent resonance structures; P has expanded octet (10e⁻); formal charges: P = 0, double-bonded O = 0, single-bonded O = -1; each P-O bond order = 1.25
worked_examples.concept_applied: Expanded octet minimizes formal charges; resonance distributes charge evenly across equivalent structures.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Draw all resonance structures for the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) and determine the bond order of each C-O bond.

step_by_step.explanation: C (4e⁻) + 3×O (18e⁻) + 2e⁻ (charge) = 24 valence electrons
C central with one C=O and two C-O bonds; complete octets
step_by_step.calculation: FC: C = 0, double-bonded O = 0, single-bonded O = -1 each

step_by_step.explanation: 3 equivalent structures (double bond on each of 3 O atoms)
Each C-O bond is double in 1 structure, single in 2 structures; average = (2 + 1 + 1)/3 = 4/3
step_by_step.calculation: Bond order = 1⅓ for each C-O bond

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₃²⁻: 3 equivalent resonance structures; each C-O bond order = 1⅓; all C-O bonds identical in length and strength
worked_examples.concept_applied: Equivalent resonance structures contribute equally; bond order is average of contributing structures.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Explain why BF₃ has an incomplete octet on boron while SF₆ has an expanded octet on sulfur.

step_by_step.explanation: B is period 2, Group 13; has 3 valence electrons; forms 3 bonds (6e⁻ total)
step_by_step.calculation: B cannot expand octet (no d orbitals in period 2)

step_by_step.explanation: S is period 3, Group 16; has 6 valence electrons; can use 3d orbitals for bonding
step_by_step.calculation: S can expand octet (has d orbitals available)

step_by_step.explanation: Period 2 elements (B, C, N, O, F): max 8e⁻; Period 3+ elements (S, P, Cl, etc.): can exceed 8e⁻
Incomplete octets common for Group 13 (B, Al); expanded octets only for period 3+ elements
step_by_step.calculation: BF₃: 6e⁻ on B (incomplete); SF₆: 12e⁻ on S (expanded)

worked_examples.final_answer: BF₃ has incomplete octet (6e⁻ on B) because B is period 2 and cannot expand octet; SF₆ has expanded octet (12e⁻ on S) because S is period 3 with available d orbitals for bonding
worked_examples.concept_applied: Period 2 elements cannot expand octet (no d orbitals); period 3+ elements can expand octet using d orbitals.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for the nitrite ion (NO₂⁻) and explain why it has resonance.

step_by_step.explanation: N (5e⁻) + 2×O (12e⁻) + 1e⁻ (charge) = 18 valence electrons
N central with 2 O atoms; 2 N-O bonds (4e⁻); 14e⁻ remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Complete O octets first (12e⁻); 2e⁻ left for N lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: N has only 6e⁻ (2 bonds + 1 lone pair); form N=O double bond
Double bond can be on either O atom; 2 equivalent resonance structures
Multiple valid electron arrangements with same connectivity; electrons delocalized over both N-O bonds

worked_examples.final_answer: NO₂⁻: N central with one N=O and one N-O bond in each resonance structure; 2 equivalent resonance structures; N has 1 lone pair; each N-O bond order = 1.5; resonance occurs because double bond can be on either O atom with equivalent energy
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance occurs when multiple valid electron arrangements exist with same atomic connectivity; electrons are delocalized.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Determine the formal charge on each atom in the ammonium ion (NH₄⁺) and verify the sum equals the ionic charge.

step_by_step.explanation: N central with 4 N-H single bonds; no lone pairs on N
V = 5 (N valence); L = 0 (no lone pairs); B = 8 (4 bonds × 2e⁻)
V = 1 (H valence); L = 0 (no lone pairs); B = 2 (1 bond × 2e⁻)
Sum all formal charges: (+1) + 4(0) = +1

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₄⁺: N formal charge = +1; each H formal charge = 0; sum = +1 (matches ionic charge); N has 4 bonds and no lone pairs
worked_examples.concept_applied: Formal charge calculation verifies Lewis structure correctness; sum must equal molecular/ionic charge.

summary: Lewis structures for complex molecules require systematic application of: (1) valence electron counting (include ionic charges); (2) correct atomic connectivity (H always terminal, least EN atom central); (3) electron distribution (terminal atoms first); (4) multiple bond formation if central atom lacks octet; (5) formal charge calculation and minimization (FC = V - L - B/2); (6) resonance structure identification (only electrons move, not atoms); (7) octet exception recognition (incomplete for B, Be, Al; expanded for period 3+; odd-electron for radicals). Formal charge guidelines: minimize FC, place negative FC on more EN atoms, minimize charge separation. Resonance: equivalent structures contribute equally (NO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, benzene); non-equivalent structures contribute unequally based on FC and EN. Expanded octets: only period 3+ elements (S, P, Cl, Xe) using d orbitals; reduces formal charges (SO₄²⁻, ClO₄⁻, SF₆). Incomplete octets: Group 13 elements (B, Al) stable with 6e⁻; act as Lewis acids. Odd-electron species: radicals with unpaired electrons (NO, NO₂); highly reactive. Systematic 7-step approach ensures accurate structures: count electrons, determine connectivity, distribute electrons, place on central, form multiple bonds, minimize formal charges, check resonance/octet exceptions. Common errors: incorrect electron count (forget ionic charge), wrong central atom (H in center), ignoring formal charge, misunderstanding resonance (molecules don't switch structures), expanding octet for period 2 elements, moving atoms in resonance. Mastery enables accurate representation of bonding in complex molecules, ions, and resonance-stabilized species.
